title: External wall insulation: what the process involves
subtitle: A decision guide for solid-wall homes considering EWI
excerpt: External wall insulation wraps the outside of a solid-walled house in insulation and render, reducing heat loss through the walls by 50-70%. The work is nearly always installer work at significant cost. This is the decision guide.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Part L1B (2022) for U-value targets. CIBSE guide on EWI and condensation risk. Energy Saving Trust solid-wall insulation guidance. Historic England guidance on EWI for listed and traditional buildings.

content.text: Solid-wall homes (typically pre-1930s UK construction, built with a single leaf of brick or stone) lose roughly twice as much heat through their walls as cavity-wall homes of the same period. The wall
U-value

of a 220 mm solid brick wall is approximately 2.1 W/m²·K. The Part L renovation target for solid walls is 0.30 W/m²·K. External wall insulation (EWI) is the method that achieves the biggest improvement while improving the appearance of the building and not reducing internal room size.
What EWI involves

The work happens on the outside of the building. The installer fixes insulation boards to the outer face of the wall with adhesive and mechanical fixings. Common board types are expanded polystyrene (EPS), mineral wool slabs, and phenolic boards. A reinforcing mesh is set into a base coat, then a render or cladding finish goes over the top. Anything that sticks out from the wall (windows, doors, soffits) needs extended sills, cavity closers, and careful detailing around every fixing.
The thermal advantage over internal wall insulation (IWI) is continuity. EWI wraps the facade without gaps at floor junctions, structural beams, or wall ties, eliminating the repeating
thermal bridges
that IWI cannot address. It also moves the wall itself onto the warm side of the insulation: the wall stores heat more effectively and is protected from freeze-thaw cycling. There is no
vapour control layer
requirement on the internal face.
The planning question
EWI thickens the building by 80-250 mm on each external face, changes the facade appearance, and may affect the building line on street-facing walls. The key planning questions for any property:

Listed building: EWI is almost certainly not permitted on a listed building. The internal option (IWI) is the only retrofit route, and even that requires listed-building consent. Check with the local planning authority before commissioning any survey.
Conservation area: EWI on the front of the house usually needs planning permission. It changes how the building looks. Rear and side walls are sometimes permitted development.

Standard permitted development (outside listed or conservation areas): EWI on a rear or side wall is usually permitted development. A front install that changes the building's material or alters its look usually needs permission.
The worked example

A 1905 Victorian terrace in Bristol: three-bedroom mid-terrace, 100 m² floor area, 70 m² of exposed external wall. Wall U-value before: 2.1 W/m²·K. After 90 mm EPS EWI: 0.30 W/m²·K. Heat loss through walls reduces by approximately 85% (from 2.1 to 0.30 on the same 70 m²). At a 10°C temperature difference (average UK heating season), the wall heat loss reduces from approximately 1470 W to 210 W. Over a five-month heating season, that is approximately 1900 kWh saved per year. At current gas prices, the annual saving is modest. The installed cost for the rear and side walls is significant. Financial payback on energy savings alone is 70-105 years at current gas rates.

attrs.body: EWI rarely pays back on energy savings alone at current gas prices. The case is environmental (large absolute carbon reduction for a hard-to-insulate house type), comfort (cold walls cause radiant discomfort even with adequate air temperature), and building condition (protected walls last longer). ECO4 and GBIS schemes may fund EWI for eligible households. The BUS grant does not cover EWI.
attrs.title: The financial case at current UK gas rates

content.text: The moisture question

Solid-wall houses in the UK are often breathable. Traditional lime-mortared brick walls move moisture in and out. Some EWI systems are not breathable; others use mineral wool with breathable renders. For a lime-mortared or stone house, specifying a breathable EWI system (mineral wool boards with mineral render, not EPS with a polymer coat) avoids trapping moisture in the wall. The installer must assess the wall's moisture behaviour before specifying the system.
What to check before commissioning
Planning permission status: check which walls you can treat without permission before you get quotes.
INCA membership: EWI fitters should be INCA members. INCA is the trade body for render and cladding. Any public scheme needs INCA members.
Damp survey first: deal with any rising damp, penetrating damp, or cavity bridging before the outside is sealed up.
Party wall agreement: if the EWI continues to the party wall, a party wall surveyor's input may be required.
What solid wall insulation looks like done well vs done badly

Done well, the EWI is flush. The render colour and texture are even. Extended window sills and door surrounds sit level and are properly sealed. All pipes and cables are sealed back to the new surface. Each window reveal is filled with insulation, so there is no cold bridge at the frame. Done badly, the render cracks over boards that did not bond. Reveals show as cold lines on a thermal image. The junctions at the roof verge are poorly detailed. Window sills are too short and water runs in behind the system.
The
solid wall insulation
decision is large and irreversible. A PAS 2035-aligned whole-house survey is the right starting point before commissioning any work.
